items.title: WHO holds briefing about Ebola outbreak
items.summary_auto: WHO Director-General Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus and colleagues are taking questions about an ongoing outbreak of Ebola virus that has seen cases reported in the Democratic Republic of Congo and Uganda.

items.title: More heat records expected as deadly 'Omega' heat wave grips Europe
items.summary_auto: Western Europe was in the grip of a heat wave on Wednesday that shattered records, caused the deaths of dozens of people, closed schools, knocked out electricity and wiped out poultry farms.

items.title: Love Story or Hoax? All of the Taylor Swift-Travis Kelce wedding rumours
items.summary_auto: It’s a date likely etched into the minds of many Swifties: Aug. 26, 2025 — the day Taylor Swift and Travis Kelce announced their engagement on Instagram. Since then, fans have been fixated on uncovering possible details about when and where the wedding may take place.

items.title: How Saskatchewan's new ballroom scene is creating a fresh community for local street dancers
items.summary_auto: Saskatoon based multi-disciplinary artist Aldeneil Española has worked for three years to launch Saskatchewan's inaugural Prairie Kiki Ball, a grassroots effort to establish a permanent ballroom community in the province.

items.title: Sask. veterinarian recruits chatty sheep to be blind llama's bestie
items.summary_auto: A Saskatchewan veterinarian went on a search to find her newly blind llama named Maple, a companion to help her adjust to her new life. That new companion came in the form of Peeps the sheep.

items.title: Ukraine launches drone offensive in Crimea and inside Russia
items.summary_auto: Ukrainian drones knocked out power in the biggest city in Russian-held Crimea on Wednesday and targeted facilities ‌in central and southern Russia, underscoring the reach of Kyiv's attacks on energy infrastructure.

items.title: Needing only a draw, Canada coach cautions against playing safe in Group B finale vs. Switzerland
Study examines how many YouTubers offering nutrition advice are actually qualified to give it
items.summary_auto: Registered dietitian Leah Cahill says people come to her all the time asking about nutrition information they've seen on social media. She is one of the authors of a new study looking at the quality of that influencer advice.

items.title: Abuse alleged at N.L. preschool as parents demand answers
items.summary_auto: Parents of children who attended a pre-kindergarten program in eastern Newfoundland say their kids were knelt on, yelled at, and locked in a bathroom by an early childhood educator who police investigated but never charged.

items.title: Does it seem easier to book a dermatologist for Botox than a mole check? You're not alone
items.summary_auto: Across the country, Canadians are waiting months to get access to a dermatologist for medical purposes, including skin checks, mole removals, eczema and psoriasis.

items.title: Canada's AI strategy aims to make health data more accessible. How will it protect privacy?
items.summary_auto: Connecting health data across the country will help researchers conduct clinical trials, test AI health tools and drive innovation in treatment and diagnosis, says federal Minister of Artificial Intelligence Evan Solomon. But the initiative will also have to balance privacy considerations.

items.title: India’s Cockroach Party shows frustrated Gen-Zers are hard to crush
items.summary_auto: India’s Cockroach Janta Party has extended its protest in Delhi to a fourth day, as it demands the resignation of the country's education minister. The CJP, which emerged just over a month ago, is aimed at India’s bulging youth demographic, which is frustrated over a lack of jobs and a test scandal in education.

items.title: Orillia, Ont., to decide fate of controversial Samuel de Champlain statue
items.summary_auto: As the central Ontario city of Orillia decides what will happen to an imposing, century-old statue of Samuel de Champlain, it remains hidden away in storage. The city council will choose to either restore it, modify it, send it away or melt it down.

items.title: Why a 25% discount on airfare cost a Montreal couple more — thanks to dynamic pricing
items.summary_auto: A Montreal couple was confused and upset after they rebooked their Air Canada flight to take advantage of a new sale, and the rebooked tickets cost slightly more. Dynamic pricing, which can cause base fares to fluctuate, was partly to blame. Some experts are calling for more transparency when companies use dynamic pricing during a discount promotion.

items.title: StubHub cancels thousands of World Cup tickets, leaving fans furious and heartbroken
items.summary_auto: StubHub is facing thousands of angry consumer complaints after cancelling World Cup tickets hours before game time.

items.title: Trump threatened new tolls in the Strait of Hormuz. What if other countries follow suit?
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Donald Trump threatened to impose new tolls in the Strait of Hormuz last weekend, sparking worries that the practice of charging to pass through critical waterways could spread — with devastating consequences to the global economy. Iran has blocked the strait for much of the war, reportedly charging tolls as high as $2 million US for ships it allows to pass.

items.title: Wizards make AJ Dybantsa top pick in NBA Draft, Raptors take Allen Graves at No. 19
items.summary_auto: The Washington Wizards selected forward AJ Dybantsa, who led the nation in scoring in his one season at BYU, with the No. 1 pick in the NBA draft on Tuesday night, while the Toronto Raptors selected forward Allen Graves 19th overall.

items.title: Israel committed genocide in Gaza, deliberately targeted Palestinian children: UN report
items.summary_auto: The UN's Commission of Inquiry found that Israel had genocidal intent in the Gaza Strip during the war, with the key indicator being its targeting of children. The Gaza Health ministry says that since the ceasefire began in October 2025, more than 1,000 people died, 265 of them children.

items.title: Regina mosque faces threats after launching weekly amplified call to prayer
items.summary_auto: A downtown Regina mosque says it has received threats after beginning a weekly amplified Muslim call to prayer from its rooftop speaker. Police said the permit is based on Regina's noise abatement bylaw and is in place until July 10, 2026, when the city and police will review the application.

items.title: Immigration minister says 'something' happened to trigger order to surrender citizenship docs
items.summary_auto: Immigration Minister Lena Diab said she told her department to investigate when she became aware that "something" had happened to trigger a wave of citizenship document recalls.

items.title: Years in the making: An inside look at the PWHL's 1st outside investment
items.summary_auto: A "belief in the growth of the game" is behind a "significant" investment in the PWHL from the Detroit-based Ilitch Companies, according to a company executive. The Ilitch Companies and Toronto businessman Larry Tanenbaum's Kilmer Sports Ventures were announced as the first outside investors in the PWHL earlier this week.

items.title: First Nation demands Carney apology amid 60-year mercury crisis
items.summary_auto: Grassy Narrows First Nation is demanding an apology from Prime Minister Mark Carney, and for a paper mill linked to mercury contamination to be closed. Protesters say the Dryden paper mill, which dumped about nine tonnes of mercury into the English-Wabigoon River System in the 1960s and '70s, continues to pollute their community's water system.

items.title: Calgary Stampede party tent noise dispute has been cranked to 11
items.summary_auto: Danielle Smith's government put much of its weight behind Cowboys Music Festival's demand for later weeknight curfews. City council barely budged.

items.title: Needing only a draw, Canada coach Marsch cautions against playing safe in Group B finale vs. Switzerland
items.summary_auto: Canada coach Jesse Marsch suggested he’s not going to ask his team to stray from its relentless, baked-in style — even if a tie, if just this once, would be victory enough when it plays Switzerland on Wednesday.

items.title: This Ontario city's set for final vote on a data centre moratorium. Here's more on the debate
items.summary_auto: Hamilton city council is poised to decide whether to approve a moratorium on data centre development pending new local rules. Proponents say it'd give the city time to respond to a growing industry. Critics worry developments would be driven to other jurisdictions and there'd be missed business opportunities.

items.title: Winnipeg outreach worker wrongly detained after fatal stabbing says he feels he was racially profiled
items.summary_auto: A community health worker who was detained near the scene of a fatal stabbing last week says he believes he might have been a victim of racial profiling.

items.title: Steve Nash encourages Canadian men's team ahead of World Cup match against Switzerland
items.summary_auto: Ahead of Canada's group-stage finale at the World Cup, the team got a bit of encouragement from a Canadian sporting hero. Two-time NBA MVP Steve Nash sent a message to the team on Tuesday before Canada's match against Switzerland on Wednesday, coach Jesse Marsch said.

items.title: New N.W.T. highway set to be Carney government's 1st national interest project: sources
items.summary_auto: CBC News has learned the federal government is expected to announce on Wednesday that it is planning to list the Mackenzie Valley Highway in the Northwest Territories as a project of national interest.

items.title: The Reflecting Pool saga | About That
items.summary_auto: U.S. President Donald Trump's attempt to repair and repaint the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool in Washington, D.C., is not going as planned. Andrew Chang breaks down the race to fix a slew of problems by Independence Day, from large swaths of peeling paint to swampy algae blooms. (Photo credits: The Canadian Press, Reuters, Adobe Stock and Getty Images)

items.title: Canadian Army battalion commander, chief warrant officer removed amid investigation
items.summary_auto: The Canadian Army has sidelined the commanding officer and chief warrant officer of the Edmonton-based battalion. Defence officials will not disclose the reasons, but current and former military sources point to an alleged alcohol-related incident.

items.title: N.S. Tories add to their supermajority with byelection win
items.summary_auto: The Nova Scotia Progressive Conservatives just picked up the province's newest and smallest riding with 48.76 per cent of the vote.

items.title: U.S. levies sanctions on Cuban state companies that are key to island nation's economy
items.summary_auto: The U.S. hit Cuban state companies on Tuesday with new sanctions that analysts say are expected to spook foreign investors and deepen a severe economic crisis.

items.title: Provincial auditor urges Sask. to include all expected wildfire costs in budgeting
items.summary_auto: In each of the past three years, the Saskatchewan Public Safety Agency's costs to respond to wildfires has exceeded its initial budget by more than $70 million.

items.title: No Quebec English leaders' debate before provincial election as 2 parties decline invite
items.summary_auto: There will not be an English-language debate before the upcoming Quebec provincial election, after two of the main parties declined an invitation.

items.title: Tesla being investigated after car drives into home, killing 76-year-old woman
items.summary_auto: The top U.S. auto regulator opened an investigation Monday after a Tesla using an automated driving feature slammed into a Texas home at high speed and killed a 76-year-old woman standing inside.

items.title: The World Cup brings Africa to life in Toronto
items.summary_auto: Three of Toronto's five World Cup group-stage matches feature African teams. Local fans and some of Ghana's soccer stars weigh in on this 'once-in-a-lifetime' experience.

items.title: Five Eyes cybersecurity agencies warn of new AI models impact on cyber risks
items.summary_auto: Cutting-edge artificial intelligence technology is poised to supercharge offensive hacking capabilities, and urgent action is needed to face up to the ‌threat, U.S., British, Canadian, Australian and New Zealand cybersecurity agency officials said on Monday.

items.title: Brexit, 10 years later: What a regret-fuelled British town can teach Canada about referendums
items.summary_auto: A decade after Britain voted to leave Europe, neither side is happy, offering a cautionary tale for Canadians about the risks of referendums.

items.title: Jérémy Doku left the World Cup for the birth of his 1st child, and people had opinions
items.summary_auto: Belgium winger Jérémy Doku flew from the World Cup to be with his wife for the birth of their son on Sunday, sparking a debate about professional athletes and fatherhood.

items.title: Ronaldo makes World Cup history with 2-goal effort in Portugal's 5-0 win against Uzbekistan
items.summary_auto: Cristiano Ronaldo scored two goals to help Portugal roll to a 5-0 win over Uzbekistan at the World Cup on Tuesday in Houston, bouncing back after a disappointing performance in the team's opening match.
